item.title: I hated using Google Chrome until a tiny adjustment changed everything
item.itunes:title: I hated using Google Chrome until a tiny adjustment changed everything
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When I’m deep in research, I usually have twenty tabs open. After a while, the favicons disappear and Chrome leaves me with a row of anonymous gray slivers. This was my daily frustration with Chrome for years. Whether I was working on a 32-inch Windows desktop or trying to stay productive on a 14-inch MacBook Pro, the horizontal tab bar remained the ultimate bottleneck.

Although I'm a big fan of Chrome, I was considering switching to a different browser simply because it doesn't offer vertical tabs. And I found this hidden setting, and it changed everything.

5 Microsoft Edge tab tricks for Windows power users

Conquer the chaos of your tabs

4
By  Anurag Singh

I prefer Google Chrome

For obvious reasons

You might ask why I didn't simply choose another web browser with vertical tabs. I tried. I looked at Edge and Brave, both of which have had vertical tabs baked into their DNA for a while now.

But every time I drifted away, I found myself pulled back to Chrome for obvious reasons. There is a snappiness to Chrome that I have never quite found elsewhere.

When I’m bouncing between high-intensity tasks, I need a browser that doesn’t just keep up but stays out of the way. Chrome is that old reliable that somehow stays the fastest in the race.

I also prefer a good design. The Material You makeover in Chrome is beautiful. The way the UI colors subtly shift to match my setup creates a polished environment. It’s clean, modern, and makes looking at a screen for ten hours a day a lot more pleasant.

I loved Gemini’s tight integration in Chrome. Since it works well with all other Google services like Keep Notes, Tasks, Docs, and more, using it in my workflow has been a huge productivity booster.

Overall, I didn’t want a new browser; I just wanted my favorite browser to work better on my screens.

Enabling vertical tabs in Chrome

Takes a minute

I have been tracking this feature for a while now. I saw the early rumors that Google was finally playing with a vertical layout in the Chrome Beta and Canary builds. But as much as I wanted it, I just couldn’t bring myself to switch.

When you are in the middle of a high-stakes workday, the last thing you want is a beta browser crashing or a plugin failing right before a deadline. I just needed that stable, reliable Chrome experience, and I wanted the tabs on the side.

The moment I found out, I didn’t hesitate. I didn’t have to download an unstable version or jump through hoops. I typed chrome://flags into my address bar, searched for #vertical-tabs, and flipped the switch to Enabled.

After a quick relaunch, a simple right-click on the tab bar gave me the option to move all my tabs to the side. Suddenly, that cramped, horizontal strip at the top disappeared, and my browser finally felt like it belonged on my 32-inch monitor.

The workflow boost is massive

Obvious advantages

Once I made the switch, the benefits were immediate. Here is why the vertical layout is a total game-changer for my daily flow.

I used to look at my 32-inch monitor and feel like I was wasting half of what I paid for. Most websites are designed with a vertical column of content in the center, which leaves massive space on the left and right.

Meanwhile, the horizontal tab bar at the top was stealing precious vertical pixels. By moving my tabs to the side, I was finally using that dead horizontal space and reclaiming the vertical room that actually matters.

And thanks to a vertical bar, I don’t have to guess which icon is which. I can actually read the full page titles. I’m no longer hunting for a specific Google Doc by hovering over six identical blue icons.

Subscribe to the newsletter for Chrome vertical tabs help

Join the newsletter for step-by-step Chrome vertical-tabs guidance, hidden chrome://flags tips, and practical workflows across browser productivity. Subscribe for actionable how-tos and setup walkthroughs that show how to reclaim vertical space and organize dozens of tabs.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

It’s a rare ‘one-size-fits-all’ solution that actually works for both a massive workstation and a small laptop.

When I use tab groups, they don’t just combine tabs together horizontally. They create clear, labeled sections that make sense. I can collapse a whole project’s worth of tabs into a single header and keep the sidebar clean and my focus sharp.

5 reasons I use Chrome's built-in password manager

It's convenience over privacy for me

6
By  Sumukh Rao

My Chrome love-hate relationship

It’s rare that a single UI change can solve problems, but vertical tabs managed to do exactly that. It turned my 32-inch monitor into a productivity powerhouse and rescued my 14-inch laptop from the brink of total clutter.

After all, in a world of widescreen displays and infinite scrolling, that tiny horizontal strip just doesn’t cut it anymore. If you are tired of dealing with dozens of tabs, give vertical tabs a try for just 24 hours.

It might feel a little ‘wrong’ for the first twenty minutes, but once you experience the ease of reading full-page titles and reclaimed vertical space, going back to the old method is impossible.

Google Chrome

Google Chrome is the leading desktop browser out there.

Download at Google

item.title: The best thing about modern Linux isn't the desktop, it's how easy it is to roll back your mistakes
item.itunes:title: The best thing about modern Linux isn't the desktop, it's how easy it is to roll back your mistakes
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When I made the move from Windows to Linux, I wasn't quite sure what to expect. I knew I was moving into a whole new ecosystem, and I had an idea as to what I wanted to test out, but other than that, I had no idea what I was getting into. So, I just sort of jumped in and gave Linux a try.

Fortunately, my experience with the FOSS operating system landscape was really positive, and I've stuck with Linux ever since. However, I've come to realise that one of the biggest parts of Linux that I never expected to love as much as I do is the rollback tools available to you, especially the ones you don't even need to set up.

My love began with Fedora's DNF kernel history

A small but powerful feature

One of the very first distros I tried was Fedora KDE, because I really wanted to give KDE Plasma a try, and people said that Fedora was a good pick for KDE and bleeding-edge features, so I gave it a go.

After a few days of use, I noticed something odd. When I booted up Fedora, I noticed that GRUB was listing the last three Linux kernels I had installed on my PC. It turns out that Fedora's package manager, DNF, does this by itself after an update. It's worth noting that I didn't tell Fedora to do this, nor did I have to set anything up; it just began storing past copies of the Linux kernel by itself. I knew it would come in handy one day, so I left it.

Sure enough, one update caused my KDE Plasma desktop to go into a bootloop, so I restarted my PC and loaded up a previous kernel from GRUB. Sure enough, everything was fixed, and I just had to wait for another update to fix the bootloop. It was really cool to see Fedora automatically setting up a history of kernels without my intervention for just such an occasion.

I use Rescuezilla to swap between operating systems and I love it

Distro hopping just got a lot easier.

12
By  Simon Batt

OpenSUSE's Snapper really hammered the concept home

It was a real turning point for my thoughts on Linux rollback features

Credit: openSUSE, Google Gemini

After using Fedora for a few months, I made the jump over to openSUSE Tumbleweed. OpenSUSE's pride and joy is its Snapper integration, which, much like Fedora's DNF kernel backups, worked right out of the box without me needing to lift a finger. However, unlike Fedora's DNF, Snapper was a lot more powerful.

I noticed that Snapper would automatically make a snapshot at key moments. Not just when I updated the system, but also when I made a big system change or installed a new app. Even better, Snapper would automatically make a new image immediately before and after the change happened, meaning I was never stuck without a point I could go back to if something went wrong.

Sure enough, at one point, I made some changes that I ended up regretting, and reverting what I did was as simple as getting Snapper open, identifying the snapshot that openSUSE took right before I made the changes, and rolling back to it. If I had messed up so badly that I couldn't even boot to the desktop, Snapper also adds the image list to the GRUB menu for easy booting.

I switched to Linux for unconventional reasons, and now I can't go back

Linux is simply better for my wacky experiments

By  Ayush Pande

Linux has done an amazing job of allowing people to roll back mistakes

And I wish other operating systems did something similar

So, why do I think that these rollback tools are the best part of Linux? Well, as you may have noticed from my previous experiences, both of the tools I used had two similarities: they were very powerful, and they were automatic.

I don't think I'd be writing this article if either of those two things weren't true. If Linux had backup tools that were mighty but required a ton of setup, I wouldn't be so passionate about them. Likewise, if they automatically fired but were practically useless when it came to reverting a mistake, they wouldn't be worth my time.

The fact of the matter is, a total Linux newbie such as myself can throw an operating system on my PC, muck up a ton of settings, break lots of stuff, and it doesn't matter. The operating system had already been customized to automatically keep snapshots ready just in case a sucker like me would end up ruining everything. And that makes Linux's rollback tools one of its best elements.

I switched from GNOME to KDE Plasma 6.6 and it fixed all my complaints about the Linux desktop

It's time to move on from GNOME

20
By  João Carrasqueira

Linux is surprisingly resilient to mistakes

While Linux allows for a ton of customization, it also opens the doors for people to break things a lot. Fortunately, Linux developers have decades of experience breaking their distros to the point where they've made some excellent tools that can remedy the issue.

item.title: I didn’t know I could give my old laptop a second life with an eGPU
item.itunes:title: I didn’t know I could give my old laptop a second life with an eGPU
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Let’s be honest, we’ve all been here. You had that one laptop that could run every task comfortably and just cruise through anything you threw at it. After a while, you open a few tabs, maybe Spotify in the background, maybe Discord, maybe a lightweight game you know, and suddenly the fans sound like they’re trying to take off. The laptop still works, but it doesn’t feel capable anymore. Just tired.

I used to think the same way. Once a laptop’s graphics performance was outdated, that was it. End of story. But what if it just needed an upgrade you never knew existed? I didn’t realize an eGPU could change the whole conversation.

If you’ve never looked into one before, an external GPU (eGPU) is basically a desktop graphics card that sits in a special enclosure outside your laptop and connects through high-speed ports like Thunderbolt or USB4. But there’s a catch, we’ll get to that.

3 ways to turn an old GPU into your own eGPU

Give your laptop or PC gaming handheld some extra graphical oomph.

2
By  Joe Rice-Jones

The hidden bottleneck: Why your laptop feels “tired”

Don’t mistake a weak GPU for a dead computer

Most laptops don’t fall apart all at once. In many cases, the processor is still fine, the storage still holds up, and the laptop handles everyday tasks without much trouble. The problem comes when you want them to do more than integrated graphics were ever designed for.

That’s exactly what happened to me. Slow editing timelines, games stuttering or outright crashing, the whole system feeling overwhelmed the moment anything visual got demanding. I opened Task Manager one day and there it was, the GPU sitting at 100%, completely maxed out, and still not delivering. The rest of the machine was fine. The graphics were the choke point.

That’s when an eGPU started making a lot of sense. If I could target the one weakness making my laptop feel outdated rather than replacing the whole machine, why wouldn’t I? The challenge with laptops is that they aren’t like desktops where you can swap components in and out. Most things are soldered to the board. But graphics? That turned out to be the one problem I could actually solve.

Enter the eGPU: The desktop power-up you didn't know existed

Unlocking the secret power of your Thunderbolt

I always assumed laptops weren’t really upgradeable beyond maybe storage or RAM. I definitely didn’t expect a Thunderbolt port to be the thing that changed that. If you have Thunderbolt 3, Thunderbolt 4 or USB4, you’re sitting on something much more powerful than you realize. You can connect your laptop to an external enclosure that houses a full desktop graphics card. In simple terms, it gives your laptop access to desktop GPU power without needing to open anything up or become a builder.

When I actually tried it, it felt exactly like giving my laptop a new life. A machine that struggled with games, editing, or multitasking could suddenly do a lot more than it ever could before. It’s not about boosting frame rates. It's about expanding what the machine can do before it retires.

Something else that gets overlooked is the desktop setup angle. Plug into an eGPU, connect a proper monitor or two, grab a wireless keyboard and mouse, and the experience of sitting at your desk becomes different. The same laptop that was barely coping before can now drive multiple displays and handle work you’d normally reserve for a desktop. You’re not replacing the machine, you’re fixing the one part that fell behind.

Why upgrading beats replacing and saves your wallet

Portability meets performance

I came to realize how often people nowadays feel forced into buying a whole new machine because a brand has released something newer with shinier specs, yet the old one is still functioning perfectly well. If your laptop still has a good keyboard, a decent screen, a reliable battery, and a processor that isn’t ancient, you’re throwing away a machine that still does most things well just because it can’t keep up graphically.

A laptop gives you the advantage of portability for school and everyday use. But when I want more power, I get home, plug into my desk setup and unlock a completely different level of performance. That’s the appeal of it: one machine that can play two roles.

That flexibility is a game changer, something desktops can’t achieve. A desktop is powerful in one place and useless everywhere else. An eGPU can’t fully replace a desktop, but it gives you a genuinely capable option at your desk without sacrificing the portability you already have.

The reality check: It’s not magic, and it has a catch

Compatibility, overhead, and managing expectations

EGPUs can be genuinely exciting, but you have to be realistic. Before anything else, check whether this is actually useful for your situation, or whether a new laptop makes more sense. Not every laptop will support it. Just because a laptop has a USB-C port doesn’t mean it can properly handle an eGPU; you need a Thunderbolt‑ or USB4‑enabled port that can tunnel PCIe, which not all USB‑C ports support. I learned this the hard way with an older laptop I owned. It had USB-C. I assumed it would work, and the performance gain was basically nothing. The port looked right, but it wasn’t Thunderbolt‑ or USB4‑compatible, which means it can’t tunnel PCIe and therefore can’t support an eGPU.

Subscribe for eGPU and laptop upgrade insights

Want to know if an eGPU can revive your laptop? Subscribe to the newsletter for step-by-step guides, compatibility checklists, and real-world setup tips that make it easier to evaluate external GPUs and other practical hardware upgrades.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Even with the right connection, an eGPU won’t perform identically to the same card inside a proper desktop. There’s overhead from running it externally, and some performance is always left on the table. That doesn’t make it pointless, but expectations need to stay realistic. Don’t go too far with the budget by trying to force flagship desktop performance out of a machine that was never built for it.

If your laptop is too old, too weak, or missing the right connection, this isn’t the miracle fix people want it to be. But if you have an old premium laptop that could handle tasks but was just breathing heavily under the load, that’s exactly the machine an eGPU was made for.

Is it worth the investment?

I genuinely didn’t realize how practical eGPUs could be until I looked into them properly. I used to think that was the end of the road. But that’s not always true. Some of them have enough life left for everyday use, and in some other cases, much more than that. If the only thing holding them back is weak graphics performance, then an eGPU can be one of the most interesting ways to make that hardware feel useful again.

It won’t be the right call for every machine. But for the right one, it can be the difference between retirement and a few more genuinely useful years. And honestly, I think it’s a better ending for an older laptop than just letting it collect dust.

If you're serious about gaming, here's why an eGPU might be the best upgrade for your laptop

Give your laptop more graphical grunt with an eGPU.

2
By  Joe Rice-Jones
item.description:
Let’s be honest, we’ve all been here. You had that one laptop that could run every task comfortably and just cruise through anything you threw at it. After a while, you open a few tabs, maybe Spotify in the background, maybe Discord, maybe a lightweight game you know, and suddenly the fans sound like they’re trying to take off. The laptop still works, but it doesn’t feel capable anymore. Just tired.

I used to think the same way. Once a laptop’s graphics performance was outdated, that was it. End of story. But what if it just needed an upgrade you never knew existed? I didn’t realize an eGPU could change the whole conversation.

If you’ve never looked into one before, an external GPU (eGPU) is basically a desktop graphics card that sits in a special enclosure outside your laptop and connects through high-speed ports like Thunderbolt or USB4. But there’s a catch, we’ll get to that.

3 ways to turn an old GPU into your own eGPU

Give your laptop or PC gaming handheld some extra graphical oomph.

2
By  Joe Rice-Jones

The hidden bottleneck: Why your laptop feels “tired”

Don’t mistake a weak GPU for a dead computer

Most laptops don’t fall apart all at once. In many cases, the processor is still fine, the storage still holds up, and the laptop handles everyday tasks without much trouble. The problem comes when you want them to do more than integrated graphics were ever designed for.

That’s exactly what happened to me. Slow editing timelines, games stuttering or outright crashing, the whole system feeling overwhelmed the moment anything visual got demanding. I opened Task Manager one day and there it was, the GPU sitting at 100%, completely maxed out, and still not delivering. The rest of the machine was fine. The graphics were the choke point.

That’s when an eGPU started making a lot of sense. If I could target the one weakness making my laptop feel outdated rather than replacing the whole machine, why wouldn’t I? The challenge with laptops is that they aren’t like desktops where you can swap components in and out. Most things are soldered to the board. But graphics? That turned out to be the one problem I could actually solve.

Enter the eGPU: The desktop power-up you didn't know existed

Unlocking the secret power of your Thunderbolt

I always assumed laptops weren’t really upgradeable beyond maybe storage or RAM. I definitely didn’t expect a Thunderbolt port to be the thing that changed that. If you have Thunderbolt 3, Thunderbolt 4 or USB4, you’re sitting on something much more powerful than you realize. You can connect your laptop to an external enclosure that houses a full desktop graphics card. In simple terms, it gives your laptop access to desktop GPU power without needing to open anything up or become a builder.

When I actually tried it, it felt exactly like giving my laptop a new life. A machine that struggled with games, editing, or multitasking could suddenly do a lot more than it ever could before. It’s not about boosting frame rates. It's about expanding what the machine can do before it retires.

A laptop gives you the advantage of portability for school and everyday use. But when I want more power, I get home, plug into my desk setup and unlock a completely different level of performance. That’s the appeal of it: one machine that can play two roles.

That flexibility is a game changer, something desktops can’t achieve. A desktop is powerful in one place and useless everywhere else. An eGPU can’t fully replace a desktop, but it gives you a genuinely capable option at your desk without sacrificing the portability you already have.

The reality check: It’s not magic, and it has a catch

EGPUs can be genuinely exciting, but you have to be realistic. Before anything else, check whether this is actually useful for your situation, or whether a new laptop makes more sense. Not every laptop will support it. Just because a laptop has a USB-C port doesn’t mean it can properly handle an eGPU; you need a Thunderbolt‑ or USB4‑enabled port that can tunnel PCIe, which not all USB‑C ports support. I learned this the hard way with an older laptop I owned. It had USB-C. I assumed it would work, and the performance gain was basically nothing. The port looked right, but it wasn’t Thunderbolt‑ or USB4‑compatible, which means it can’t tunnel PCIe and therefore can’t support an eGPU.

Subscribe for eGPU and laptop upgrade insights

Want to know if an eGPU can revive your laptop? Subscribe to the newsletter for step-by-step guides, compatibility checklists, and real-world setup tips that make it easier to evaluate external GPUs and other practical hardware upgrades.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Even with the right connection, an eGPU won’t perform identically to the same card inside a proper desktop. There’s overhead from running it externally, and some performance is always left on the table. That doesn’t make it pointless, but expectations need to stay realistic. Don’t go too far with the budget by trying to force flagship desktop performance out of a machine that was never built for it.

If your laptop is too old, too weak, or missing the right connection, this isn’t the miracle fix people want it to be. But if you have an old premium laptop that could handle tasks but was just breathing heavily under the load, that’s exactly the machine an eGPU was made for.

Is it worth the investment?

I genuinely didn’t realize how practical eGPUs could be until I looked into them properly. I used to think that was the end of the road. But that’s not always true. Some of them have enough life left for everyday use, and in some other cases, much more than that. If the only thing holding them back is weak graphics performance, then an eGPU can be one of the most interesting ways to make that hardware feel useful again.

It won’t be the right call for every machine. But for the right one, it can be the difference between retirement and a few more genuinely useful years. And honestly, I think it’s a better ending for an older laptop than just letting it collect dust.

If you're serious about gaming, here's why an eGPU might be the best upgrade for your laptop

Give your laptop more graphical grunt with an eGPU.

2
By  Joe Rice-Jones

item.title: I replaced File Explorer with two apps for a week, and I'm not going back
item.itunes:title: I replaced File Explorer with two apps for a week, and I'm not going back
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
For the longest time, Windows users never really had a lot of choices when it came to their file manager. Sure, there have been a couple of niche file managers that programmers and power users have sworn by for a couple of decades now, but today, every kind of user is spoilt for choice.

Now, to Microsoft's credit, File Explorer is better now than it ever used to be, with its tabs and clean UI. But for Windows 11 daily drivers (over 60% of global desktop users), sluggish folder loads, inconsistent menus, and a not-so-great workflow are still friction-inducing problems to contend with. So, for an entire week, I replaced File Explorer with not one, but two alternatives, and by the end of it, I didn't expect to hesitate between them.

8 Windows features you should replace with third-party apps

You can do so much better

7
By  João Carrasqueira

File Explorer is good now, but still not enough

Microsoft has fixed the basics, but missed the experience

In the interest of complete honesty, I'll admit that File Explorer in Windows 11 isn't a complete disaster at all. In fact, with tabs, the command bar, and its overall modernizations, there isn't a lot that the average user would find fault in. However, using it every day still feels like dealing with something that's only been partially rethought, and that goes for anyone even partially on the power user spectrum.

The biggest issue has more to do with the overall experience than it has to do with any one missing feature, though. Folder navigation still has moments where it just slows down for no good reason. Before I turned off Automatic Folder Type Discovery, Explorer still liked to become lethargic out of the blue, and the context menus are definitely a problematic compromise between the old and the new, instead of feeling like a clean transition.

So, over the past few months, I caught myself working around File Explorer more than actually enjoying it. Opening multiple windows just to manage files, constantly resizing things, dealing with inconsistent layouts — it all added up to the point I found myself finally looking for alternatives.

Microsoft responds to feedback with long-requested Windows 11 features, including taskbar tweaks and improved file explorer

The tech giant says it's listening to user feedback.

4
By  Patrick O'Rourke

Files is the explorer Windows 11 should've had

It's modern, clean, and built for how we actually use Windows

The first file explorer I decided to use was Files. The moment I launched the app, it immediately felt like what Microsoft meant File Explorer to be, but never quite managed to deliver. Out of all the file managers for Windows 11 available today, Files comes the closest to looking like it's natively a part of the OS, thanks to its clean visuals and design. In fact, the design actually improves how you interact with your files. For starters, tabs in Files don't feel like an afterthought (which we know they were since Microsoft added them to Windows 11 with a later update). Instead, tabs are central to the entire experience here.

The layouts are flexible, the navigation feels so much snappier than File Explorer's, and there are small touches like tagging and better sidebar organization that genuinely make a difference in the daily user experience, especially when you're juggling multiple folders. You'd be surprised, much as I was, that using Files almost immediately becomes second nature. That ease of use comes from how everything is where you'd expect it to be, but just... better thought out. Even simple tasks like copying files, or jumping between directories, felt smoother and more intentional.

There's also a sense of cohesion in Files that File Explorer still lacks. Nothing feels bolted on. It's all consistent, visually and functionally, in a way that makes daily use feel less like a chore. So, by day three, Files had become the go-to explorer for me, before I completely switched gears and tested the second, more recommended software.

Files 4.0 just came out, and these are my 6 favorite features

A fantastic file manager gets even better

16
By  João Carrasqueira
Files
7/10

Files in an open-source file explorer for Windows that offers a customizable interface.

See at Files See at Microsoft Store

OneCommander is what happens when power users take over

Dual panes, deep control, and zero hand-holding

OneCommander, right from the get-go, feels like you're stepping into a completely different philosophy vis-à-vis file management. I'd spent half a week seeing Files refining the default experience, and in came OneCommander, throwing it out entirely and building something that gave me control over every file on my PC.

OneCommander is a pretty popular File Explorer alternative, and rightly so. The dual-pane layout is the headline feature here, and it genuinely changes how you work on Windows 11, immediately. Dragging files between directories, comparing folders, and organizing large batches are all tasks that become much faster and more deliberate, and once it clicks, going back to a single-pane view feels restrictive. I myself expected to be relieved when I switched back to a single-pane view, and yet, it almost felt like my brain itched to get the dual-pane view back.

OneCommander doesn't stop there, either. It's packed with customization options, alternative layouts like Miller columns, and the overall level of flexibility on offer here genuinely borders on overwhelming. It's almost like playing a game that doesn't hold your hand and has a steep learning curve: OneCommander expects you to meet it halfway. That's why things slowed down initially, since I had to take the time to figure out where things were, and how best to set things up. Plenty of online tutorials helped, and once everything was good to go by the end of day one, I felt like I'd switched to a maximum-efficiency tool. This is not going to feel like a familiar file manager, but OneCommander isn't trying to be one, either. It's just trying to be better, and that too, on its own terms, where it undoubtedly succeeds.

OneCommander
See at OneCommander See at Microsoft Store
6 reasons OneCommander is my favorite free file manager for Windows, and it's not even close

What's there not to love?

19
By  João Carrasqueira

The one I chose wasn't the one I expected

Files has won me over (for now)

Going into this, I fully expected to stick with OneCommander. On paper and while using it, it's clearly the more powerful tool. And yet, after a full week of actually living with both, I kept coming back to the Files app. And the more I try to come to a reason for it, the simpler it gets: Files fits into my workflow without demanding that I change it.

Now, OneCommander is absolutely brilliant when you're actively managing files, and moving, sorting, and organizing them at scale. On the other hand, Files works just as well when you're not thinking about File Management at all. It's the app I reached for when I just needed to grab something quickly, open a folder, or move between projects without breaking my flow. It's also the app that made me breathe an audible sigh of relief when I switched back to it after three days of using OneCommander.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter Windows file tips

Get deeper file-manager guidance: subscribe to the newsletter for comparisons, tips, and hands-on recommendations on Windows file apps—know when Files fits daily workflows and when OneCommander earns its place, so you can choose with confidence.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

There's a balance here that Files gets right. It significantly improves on File Explorer in meaningful ways, all without overwhelming the user with options they won't always need. Think of it more as an upgrade to the established system instead of a replacement that you have to put in effort to adapt to. It's not as efficient as OneCommander, of course, but it's certainly more consistent, and instead of maximum control, I'm going for everyday usability, since that's what I value more, for now, at least.

I fixed Windows 11 File Explorer lag by disabling this legacy service

A quarter-century old service we no longer need.

12
By  Samarveer Singh

Which one should you use?

Files gives me something better to reach for daily, even if it doesn't replace File Explorer entirely.

After a week of bouncing between both, I can state that the answer is more about which one aligns with how you work. If your day revolved around moving files in bulk, comparing folders, and managing directories, then OneCommander is, without a shadow of doubt, the one to go with. It's the more powerful tool of the two, and the dual-pane workflow and depth are genuine time savers.

However, if you want something that feels more like a natural evolution of what Windows 11 already offers, then the Files app is the easier recommendation. It's intuitive, it's polished, and it improves your day-to-day experience without asking you to relearn anything.

The one thing to keep in mind here, however, is that neither option fully replaces File Explorer at the system level. Windows still steps in when it wants to, with File Explorer. With the option I've picked, I feel like my choice hasn't been about replacing File Explorer entirely, but rather about having something better to reach for, most of the time.

OneCommander
See at OneCommander See at Microsoft Store
6 reasons OneCommander is my favorite free file manager for Windows, and it's not even close

What's there not to love?

19
By  João Carrasqueira

The one I chose wasn't the one I expected

Files has won me over (for now)

Going into this, I fully expected to stick with OneCommander. On paper and while using it, it's clearly the more powerful tool. And yet, after a full week of actually living with both, I kept coming back to the Files app. And the more I try to come to a reason for it, the simpler it gets: Files fits into my workflow without demanding that I change it.

Now, OneCommander is absolutely brilliant when you're actively managing files, and moving, sorting, and organizing them at scale. On the other hand, Files works just as well when you're not thinking about File Management at all. It's the app I reached for when I just needed to grab something quickly, open a folder, or move between projects without breaking my flow. It's also the app that made me breathe an audible sigh of relief when I switched back to it after three days of using OneCommander.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter Windows file tips

Get deeper file-manager guidance: subscribe to the newsletter for comparisons, tips, and hands-on recommendations on Windows file apps—know when Files fits daily workflows and when OneCommander earns its place, so you can choose with confidence.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

There's a balance here that Files gets right. It significantly improves on File Explorer in meaningful ways, all without overwhelming the user with options they won't always need. Think of it more as an upgrade to the established system instead of a replacement that you have to put in effort to adapt to. It's not as efficient as OneCommander, of course, but it's certainly more consistent, and instead of maximum control, I'm going for everyday usability, since that's what I value more, for now, at least.

I fixed Windows 11 File Explorer lag by disabling this legacy service

A quarter-century old service we no longer need.

12
By  Samarveer Singh

Which one should you use?

Files gives me something better to reach for daily, even if it doesn't replace File Explorer entirely.

After a week of bouncing between both, I can state that the answer is more about which one aligns with how you work. If your day revolved around moving files in bulk, comparing folders, and managing directories, then OneCommander is, without a shadow of doubt, the one to go with. It's the more powerful tool of the two, and the dual-pane workflow and depth are genuine time savers.

However, if you want something that feels more like a natural evolution of what Windows 11 already offers, then the Files app is the easier recommendation. It's intuitive, it's polished, and it improves your day-to-day experience without asking you to relearn anything.

The one thing to keep in mind here, however, is that neither option fully replaces File Explorer at the system level. Windows still steps in when it wants to, with File Explorer. With the option I've picked, I feel like my choice hasn't been about replacing File Explorer entirely, but rather about having something better to reach for, most of the time.
item.content:encoded:
For the longest time, Windows users never really had a lot of choices when it came to their file manager. Sure, there have been a couple of niche file managers that programmers and power users have sworn by for a couple of decades now, but today, every kind of user is spoilt for choice.

Now, to Microsoft's credit, File Explorer is better now than it ever used to be, with its tabs and clean UI. But for Windows 11 daily drivers (over 60% of global desktop users), sluggish folder loads, inconsistent menus, and a not-so-great workflow are still friction-inducing problems to contend with. So, for an entire week, I replaced File Explorer with not one, but two alternatives, and by the end of it, I didn't expect to hesitate between them.

8 Windows features you should replace with third-party apps

You can do so much better

7
By  João Carrasqueira

File Explorer is good now, but still not enough

Microsoft has fixed the basics, but missed the experience

In the interest of complete honesty, I'll admit that File Explorer in Windows 11 isn't a complete disaster at all. In fact, with tabs, the command bar, and its overall modernizations, there isn't a lot that the average user would find fault in. However, using it every day still feels like dealing with something that's only been partially rethought, and that goes for anyone even partially on the power user spectrum.

The biggest issue has more to do with the overall experience than it has to do with any one missing feature, though. Folder navigation still has moments where it just slows down for no good reason. Before I turned off Automatic Folder Type Discovery, Explorer still liked to become lethargic out of the blue, and the context menus are definitely a problematic compromise between the old and the new, instead of feeling like a clean transition.

So, over the past few months, I caught myself working around File Explorer more than actually enjoying it. Opening multiple windows just to manage files, constantly resizing things, dealing with inconsistent layouts — it all added up to the point I found myself finally looking for alternatives.

Microsoft responds to feedback with long-requested Windows 11 features, including taskbar tweaks and improved file explorer

The tech giant says it's listening to user feedback.

4
By  Patrick O'Rourke

Files is the explorer Windows 11 should've had

It's modern, clean, and built for how we actually use Windows

The first file explorer I decided to use was Files. The moment I launched the app, it immediately felt like what Microsoft meant File Explorer to be, but never quite managed to deliver. Out of all the file managers for Windows 11 available today, Files comes the closest to looking like it's natively a part of the OS, thanks to its clean visuals and design. In fact, the design actually improves how you interact with your files. For starters, tabs in Files don't feel like an afterthought (which we know they were since Microsoft added them to Windows 11 with a later update). Instead, tabs are central to the entire experience here.

The layouts are flexible, the navigation feels so much snappier than File Explorer's, and there are small touches like tagging and better sidebar organization that genuinely make a difference in the daily user experience, especially when you're juggling multiple folders. You'd be surprised, much as I was, that using Files almost immediately becomes second nature. That ease of use comes from how everything is where you'd expect it to be, but just... better thought out. Even simple tasks like copying files, or jumping between directories, felt smoother and more intentional.

There's also a sense of cohesion in Files that File Explorer still lacks. Nothing feels bolted on. It's all consistent, visually and functionally, in a way that makes daily use feel less like a chore. So, by day three, Files had become the go-to explorer for me, before I completely switched gears and tested the second, more recommended software.

Files 4.0 just came out, and these are my 6 favorite features

A fantastic file manager gets even better

16
By  João Carrasqueira
Files
7/10

Files in an open-source file explorer for Windows that offers a customizable interface.

See at Files See at Microsoft Store

OneCommander is what happens when power users take over

Dual panes, deep control, and zero hand-holding

OneCommander, right from the get-go, feels like you're stepping into a completely different philosophy vis-à-vis file management. I'd spent half a week seeing Files refining the default experience, and in came OneCommander, throwing it out entirely and building something that gave me control over every file on my PC.

OneCommander is a pretty popular File Explorer alternative, and rightly so. The dual-pane layout is the headline feature here, and it genuinely changes how you work on Windows 11, immediately. Dragging files between directories, comparing folders, and organizing large batches are all tasks that become much faster and more deliberate, and once it clicks, going back to a single-pane view feels restrictive. I myself expected to be relieved when I switched back to a single-pane view, and yet, it almost felt like my brain itched to get the dual-pane view back.

OneCommander doesn't stop there, either. It's packed with customization options, alternative layouts like Miller columns, and the overall level of flexibility on offer here genuinely borders on overwhelming. It's almost like playing a game that doesn't hold your hand and has a steep learning curve: OneCommander expects you to meet it halfway. That's why things slowed down initially, since I had to take the time to figure out where things were, and how best to set things up. Plenty of online tutorials helped, and once everything was good to go by the end of day one, I felt like I'd switched to a maximum-efficiency tool. This is not going to feel like a familiar file manager, but OneCommander isn't trying to be one, either. It's just trying to be better, and that too, on its own terms, where it undoubtedly succeeds.

OneCommander
See at OneCommander See at Microsoft Store
6 reasons OneCommander is my favorite free file manager for Windows, and it's not even close

What's there not to love?

19
By  João Carrasqueira

Get deeper file-manager guidance: subscribe to the newsletter for comparisons, tips, and hands-on recommendations on Windows file apps—know when Files fits daily workflows and when OneCommander earns its place, so you can choose with confidence.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

There's a balance here that Files gets right. It significantly improves on File Explorer in meaningful ways, all without overwhelming the user with options they won't always need. Think of it more as an upgrade to the established system instead of a replacement that you have to put in effort to adapt to. It's not as efficient as OneCommander, of course, but it's certainly more consistent, and instead of maximum control, I'm going for everyday usability, since that's what I value more, for now, at least.

The one thing to keep in mind here, however, is that neither option fully replaces File Explorer at the system level. Windows still steps in when it wants to, with File Explorer. With the option I've picked, I feel like my choice hasn't been about replacing File Explorer entirely, but rather about having something better to reach for, most of the time.

item.title: I stopped ignoring these underrated smart TV features, and you should too
item.itunes:title: I stopped ignoring these underrated smart TV features, and you should too
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When most people talk about smart TVs, the bulk of the attention is given to screen sizes, panel tech, variable refresh rates, and user interfaces, and not without reason. If you have a large-screen OLED TV with a variable 144Hz refresh rate and fast software, you're almost all the way there. That said, some features fly under the radar. Most people either don't know about them or think they're not worth their time. The connectivity options, HDMI ports, and automated gaming features of your smart TV are also essential to creating a seamless and satisfying experience. It's high time you stopped ignoring these features and made full use of them to get the most out of your smart TV.

This easily missed HDMI 2.1 setting unlocks your TV’s full potential

It's performance you've paid for — don't leave it on the table

By  Samarveer Singh

Ethernet port

Switch to a more stable connection

I reckon the majority of people have only ever used Wi-Fi on their smart TV. It's convenient, works great most of the time, and is something you're already familiar with. Routing an Ethernet cable from your router to the TV room isn't easy in most cases, and the benefits may not seem worth the hassle. However, I assure you that replacing your TV's wireless connection with a wired one will reap immense rewards, both instantly and in the future. No matter how fast your Wi-Fi is, it's always prone to a bit of weirdness. Streaming apps adjust the bitrate dynamically based on the status of the wireless signal they receive, which is the reason behind occasional drops in image quality. Unless you have a mesh node nearby, walls and doors can degrade the quality of the Wi-Fi signal reaching your TV.

You've probably never used your TV's Ethernet port, but it can instantly elevate your streaming experience. By eliminating wireless shenanigans, you can provide a much more stable connection to your apps without sacrificing performance. Even if your TV Ethernet is limited to 100 Mbps, that's more than enough for 4K streaming. And if you want to stream uncompressed 4K files from your media server, 100 Mbps will still not disappoint. You could also consider a USB-to-Ethernet adapter for some extra bandwidth, if your media streaming really demands it. The Ethernet port is an underused feature on most smart TVs, and almost everyone has a lot to gain by switching to a wired connection.

TP-Link USB to Ethernet Adapter
$10 $15 Save $5
$10 at Amazon
Your smart TV may be holding your entire Wi-Fi hostage

Make your Wi-Fi great again by removing your smart TV from it

7
By  Tanveer Singh

Screen mirroring

You don't know how important it is until you need it

Screen mirroring isn't something you think too much about, but it's hard to miss when you really need it. Sharing your laptop or smartphone screen on your TV may seem unimportant before you actually need to do it. Maybe you want to browse your vacation photos with your partner on the big screen, or review the month's expenses while you work on the laptop. Even something as simple as casting your phone's screen to show your partner a product you're browsing on Amazon needs reliable screen mirroring. Thankfully, most TVs have some form of screen mirroring built in. Any smart TV running Google TV supports Chromecast for native mirroring, and Apple TV has its much-celebrated AirPlay feature. Samsung's Tizen OS has Smart View, LG's webOS has Miracast and AirPlay, Roku TV OS has its own mirroring technology, and Fire TV OS also has Miracast.

Instead of installing third-party mirroring apps on your smart TV, it's worth trying out your TV's native screencasting functionality. Many Android and Google TVs are sometimes finicky with mirroring, but it's worth trying it out. In my experience, Apple TV's AirPlay has always worked perfectly whenever I want to mirror the iPhone or MacBook screen on the TV.

Smart TVs keep getting worse, so I use an old TV with a Chromecast instead

Old tech is seemingly always better

2
By  Jasmine Mannan

Enhanced HDMI format

It's rarely enabled by default

You may have been missing out on your TV's full capabilities simply because of a little-known toggle in the input settings menu. Your HDMI ports may have been operating in "Standard Format" ever since you bought the TV. Since it's not made clear that you need to switch to "Enhanced Format" in the settings, it's easy to miss out on this key feature. Without an "Enhanced HDMI" signal, your HDMI ports most likely function at a lower bandwidth, locking you out of crucial features like HDR, VRR, 4K@60Hz or 4K@120Hz, and ALLM. Each of these features is necessary to unlock your TV's full capabilities, especially for gaming. If you have a console or gaming PC connected to your smart TV, you need to switch to the Enhanced HDMI signal ASAP.

This setting may be called by a different name on your TV, depending on the manufacturer. While Sony and TCL call it "Enhanced Format" and "HDMI Enhanced Mode," respectively, LG and Samsung use the terms "HDMI Deep Color" and "Input Signal Plus," respectively. Also, you'll need to enable this setting for each HDMI mode individually.

Not all TV HDMI ports are created equal, and I found out the hard way

Most mid-range and even some high-end TVs, require you to turn on high-bandwidth.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

HDMI eARC

Don't waste your soundbar's potential

This is yet another feature that is disabled by default on most TVs. HDMI eARC, or enhanced Audio Return Channel, was an upgrade to HDMI ARC, which became popular on smart TVs in the late 2010s. While HDMI ARC is enough to reduce cable clutter by allowing your TV to send audio to your soundbar or speakers through a single HDMI cable, eARC is an extension of the feature that increases the bandwidth the audio signal can carry. This essentially enables high-bandwidth lossless audio formats such as Dolby Atmos and DTS:X for your content, provided your soundbar supports them.

If you want to enjoy uncompressed audio on your high-quality soundbar or speakers, it's important to enable eARC in your TV's audio settings. Not all HDMI ports on your TV will support eARC, so it's also imperative that you connect your audio system to the port that does. Your HDMI ports will be labeled accordingly, so a glance behind your TV is enough to determine which port to use.

Your soundbar won't talk to your TV, and manufacturers act like that's normal

HDMI eARC was supposed to simplify home theater, but unreliable handshakes, sync issues, and audio dropouts still make it feel unfinished.

5
By  Jeff Butts

Auto Low Latency Mode (ALLM)

No reason not to use it

If you have a smart TV with gaming features like 4K resolution, a high and variable refresh rate, and decent local dimming for HDR, you're probably used to turning on "Game Mode" manually each time you switch to your console or gaming PC. Enabling ALLM automates this process, so whenever your TV detects the switch from, say, Netlfix or YouTube to your PS5 or PC, it automatically activates "Game Mode", switching the TV to its lowest latency mode. This way, you aren't eating latency penalties, and your inputs appear almost instantaneously on the screen. Your TV does a lot of image processing in the background, which isn't necessary for gaming, and is rather unwanted if you want to have the best possible experience. ALLM ensures your console or PC communicates directly with the TV to remove this background processing, without you having to disable a bunch of settings every single time.

I changed a few Google TV settings, and gaming finally felt responsive

Your TV might be adding extra input lag, and fixing it is easy

By  Abid Ahsan Shanto

Smart TV features ought to be more transparent

For the average user streaming movies and shows on their smart TV, settings like ALLM may not matter all that much. However, things like Enhanced HDMI, eARC, and an Ethernet connection will benefit every type of user. These features ensure your TV doesn't suffer from dynamic bitrate downgrades, compressed audio, or limited responsiveness and color depth. For gamers, knowing which features to enable and disable is all the more important, since sacrificing any bit of image quality and latency is not acceptable.

Your HDMI ports may have been operating in "Standard Format" ever since you bought the TV. Since it's not made clear that you need to switch to "Enhanced Format" in the settings, it's easy to miss out on this key feature. Without an "Enhanced HDMI" signal, your HDMI ports most likely function at a lower bandwidth, locking you out of crucial features like HDR, VRR, 4K@60Hz or 4K@120Hz, and ALLM. Each of these features is necessary to unlock your TV's full capabilities, especially for gaming. If you have a console or gaming PC connected to your smart TV, you need to switch to the Enhanced HDMI signal ASAP.

This setting may be called by a different name on your TV, depending on the manufacturer. While Sony and TCL call it "Enhanced Format" and "HDMI Enhanced Mode," respectively, LG and Samsung use the terms "HDMI Deep Color" and "Input Signal Plus," respectively. Also, you'll need to enable this setting for each HDMI mode individually.

Not all TV HDMI ports are created equal, and I found out the hard way

Most mid-range and even some high-end TVs, require you to turn on high-bandwidth.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

HDMI eARC

Don't waste your soundbar's potential

This is yet another feature that is disabled by default on most TVs. HDMI eARC, or enhanced Audio Return Channel, was an upgrade to HDMI ARC, which became popular on smart TVs in the late 2010s. While HDMI ARC is enough to reduce cable clutter by allowing your TV to send audio to your soundbar or speakers through a single HDMI cable, eARC is an extension of the feature that increases the bandwidth the audio signal can carry. This essentially enables high-bandwidth lossless audio formats such as Dolby Atmos and DTS:X for your content, provided your soundbar supports them.

If you want to enjoy uncompressed audio on your high-quality soundbar or speakers, it's important to enable eARC in your TV's audio settings. Not all HDMI ports on your TV will support eARC, so it's also imperative that you connect your audio system to the port that does. Your HDMI ports will be labeled accordingly, so a glance behind your TV is enough to determine which port to use.

Your soundbar won't talk to your TV, and manufacturers act like that's normal

HDMI eARC was supposed to simplify home theater, but unreliable handshakes, sync issues, and audio dropouts still make it feel unfinished.

5
By  Jeff Butts

Auto Low Latency Mode (ALLM)

No reason not to use it

If you have a smart TV with gaming features like 4K resolution, a high and variable refresh rate, and decent local dimming for HDR, you're probably used to turning on "Game Mode" manually each time you switch to your console or gaming PC. Enabling ALLM automates this process, so whenever your TV detects the switch from, say, Netlfix or YouTube to your PS5 or PC, it automatically activates "Game Mode", switching the TV to its lowest latency mode. This way, you aren't eating latency penalties, and your inputs appear almost instantaneously on the screen. Your TV does a lot of image processing in the background, which isn't necessary for gaming, and is rather unwanted if you want to have the best possible experience. ALLM ensures your console or PC communicates directly with the TV to remove this background processing, without you having to disable a bunch of settings every single time.

I changed a few Google TV settings, and gaming finally felt responsive

Your TV might be adding extra input lag, and fixing it is easy

By  Abid Ahsan Shanto

Smart TV features ought to be more transparent

For the average user streaming movies and shows on their smart TV, settings like ALLM may not matter all that much. However, things like Enhanced HDMI, eARC, and an Ethernet connection will benefit every type of user. These features ensure your TV doesn't suffer from dynamic bitrate downgrades, compressed audio, or limited responsiveness and color depth. For gamers, knowing which features to enable and disable is all the more important, since sacrificing any bit of image quality and latency is not acceptable.

item.title: My smart home was a mess until I gave it a brain
item.itunes:title: My smart home was a mess until I gave it a brain
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Smart homes promise convenience, but they can easily become another system you have to manage. I reached a point where controlling my devices started taking more effort than the problems they were supposed to solve. Instead of saving time, my setup created small interruptions throughout the day that slowly reduced my productivity.

I didn’t want more devices or more apps. I wanted my smart home to quietly support my routine without constant input. That’s when I started looking for a better approach. By adding the right control layer, I was able to simplify everything and turn my smart home into something that actually helps me stay focused instead of distracting me.

The problem my smart home created

The day my smart home became a digital scavenger hunt

The trap of a "smart home" is that you often end up serving the technology instead of it serving you. I started building mine to simplify my life, but I accidentally created a digital scavenger hunt. My issue was a fragmented ecosystem: smart bulbs from one brand, motion sensors from another, and plugs scattered across a third.

Everything technically worked, but nothing worked together. I found myself juggling four different apps just to dim the lights for a movie. The real breaking point was the "Ghost Toggle" effect; because my devices weren't synced, my porch light would trigger at 3:00 AM due to a delayed routine from a completely unrelated app. Instead of saving time, my phone became a dashboard of constant interruptions—motion alerts, battery warnings, and API timeouts. My smart home had turned into a collection of isolated gadgets rather than a single intelligent system. I always felt like I was stuck in a chaotic web of conflicting commands.

Home Assistant is the control layer I was missing

One brain turned isolated gadgets into a system

I finally realized that my smart home didn’t need more gadgets; it needed a brain. After dealing with a fragmented setup for a long time, the problem became clear: I lacked a single control layer. Each brand wanted me to stay inside its own app, which meant my smart home never truly felt “smart.”

That’s where Home Assistant came in. It acted as the structural foundation I was missing, pulling every isolated device into a single, unified interface. The shift was immediate. Instead of juggling half a dozen apps, I had one local dashboard that didn’t rely on external servers or an internet connection to function. It didn’t matter if a bulb was from one brand and a sensor from another; Home Assistant saw them as equals. It felt like adding a proper operating system to my home, providing the glue that finally turned a collection of isolated gadgets into a cohesive, private, and reliable system.

I run my entire smart home from a single mini PC with Home Assistant

If you'd have told me my smart home would be controlled from one box I wouldn't have believed you

6
By  Joe Rice-Jones

My smart home setup with Home Assistant

The minimalist approach

I didn’t want to rebuild my smart home from scratch. I simply wanted to connect the devices I already owned and make them work together in a cleaner way. That’s why I set up Home Assistant on my NAS running on an old PC, which gave me a reliable system without buying any new hardware. It was a great way to reuse an unused machine and turn it into the brain of my smart home.

My setup includes smart lights, smart plugs, motion sensors, and a few environmental sensors that track temperature and humidity. Earlier, these devices lived in separate apps, but now they all appear in one unified dashboard. I can quickly check device status, control them instantly, and organize everything by rooms to keep things simple.

I intentionally kept the setup minimal. Instead of adding more gadgets, I focused on improving how the existing ones work together. With everything accessible from one place, my smart home finally feels organized, consistent, and easier to manage every day.

The automation that fixed everything

Letting my home react to me instead of controlling it

The biggest issue with my smart home was that devices worked individually but not intelligently together. I didn’t need dozens of complex automations, I just needed one that actually solved a real problem in my daily routine.

I created a simple presence-based automation that adjusts multiple devices automatically depending on whether I am home or away. Earlier, I had to manually turn off lights, check plugs, and make sure unnecessary devices were not running. Sometimes I forgot, which defeated the purpose of having a smart home.

With Home Assistant, everything now reacts based on context instead of manual control. When I leave home, selected lights turn off, idle devices stop consuming power, and the system shifts into a low-activity mode. When I return, things go back to normal without needing any interaction.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter home control

Get practical Home Assistant guides, and subscribe to the newsletter for clear step-by-step integrations, useful automation patterns, troubleshooting tips, and broader home-tech coverage that helps your devices work together.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

It removed small but constant decisions from my day. Instead of managing devices one by one, my smart home now behaves like a coordinated system that quietly takes care of repetitive tasks in the background.

I monitor my PC with Home Assistant, and it's a bigger deal than I realized

From task manager to a fully automated digital nervous system.

4
By  Yash Patel

A smarter home should give you back your time

The real benefit of a smart home isn’t novelty, it’s reduced mental load. Once everything started working as a single system, I stopped thinking about individual devices altogether. My focus shifted from managing technology to simply living with it. That’s when I realized productivity at home isn’t about doing more tasks faster, it’s about removing the small frictions that constantly interrupt your flow.

Home Assistant helped me move from reactive control to quiet reliability. Instead of adding complexity, the right control layer simplified everything. Sometimes, the smartest upgrade isn’t a new device, it’s making the ones you already own finally work together.

Home Assistant
OS
Windows, macOS, Linux
iOS compatible
Yes
Android compatible
Yes

Home Assistant is the best way to connect your smart home systems together.

See at Apple App Store See at Google Play Store See at Home Assistant

item.title: I connected Claude to Figma and it's the workflow I didn't know I was missing
item.itunes:title: I connected Claude to Figma and it's the workflow I didn't know I was missing
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
As someone who likes to stay up to date with the design world, I’ve seen a lot of content around integrating Claude with Figma lately. And it honestly feels like it’s primarily aimed at people who code - as someone who doesn’t code at all, I felt a bit left out. Setting up MCP servers and bridging your design tokens to a codebase aren’t in my wheelhouse. It’s genuinely useful stuff, but not for someone who uses Figma the way an average designer does.

What I found when I actually started testing the integration is that there’s a whole workflow here that nobody’s really talking about, and it’s built for the non-developer side of the Figma user base. You can push structured diagrams straight to FigJam, and also use Claude to build design files complete with editable layers in the design canvas. It’s a total design collaborator that can reach into your workspace. Here’s why I started using it, and what a workflow like this can actually do for your designs…

Why use Claude with Figma?

When Figma already has its own AI

Figma has been building its own AI pretty aggressively. There’s the First Draft feature for generating UI from a text prompt, text tools for rewriting copy directly in your designs, and the biggest one: Figma Make, which lets you transform design files, images, or text instructions into working prototypes through a conversational AI interface. So why bring Claude into the mix at all when Figma already has AI?

The difference is less about output quality and more about how it lets me actually work. Figma Make is a prompt-in and design-out tool. You describe something, it generates, and then you can iterate on it, which is great for speed and experimentation. But it takes a clean prompt and runs with it, whereas Claude is more of a back-and-forth. You can spend twenty minutes working through a design problem, changing directions multiple times, reference your documents, debate UX decisions, and then say “okay now put that into Figma”. The whole conversation context goes with it, whereas Figma Make doesn’t have that thread.

Claude also handles messy inputs in a way Figma’s AI doesn't. I can get really messy with disjointed ideas, spelling errors, and screenshots that don’t make sense, and Claude just gets me. It’s excellent at inferring what I’m trying to convey and hands me something structured back.

I set up Claude Code the way its creator does, and the difference is night and day

Who better to learn from than the person who made it?

18
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Setting up Figma with Claude

There’s nothing technical about it

Again, I’m not talking about setting up an MCP server here, but simply integrating Figma with a Claude.ai connector. It does use MCP under the hood, but Anthropic hosts and manages it for you. There are a few things worth knowing before getting started: you will need a Claude Pro account, unfortunately, since the connector isn’t usable on the free tier. But a free Figma account is fine though.

In Claude, go to Settings > Connectors, browse the Figma connector, hit Connect, and follow the authentication prompts to grant Claude access to your account. It’ll open a Figma login in the browser, click Allow, and that’s pretty much it. You can configure the permissions if needed. Then it’s just a matter of prompting Claude for whatever you want.

Here’s what you can do with the Claude Figma connector:

  • Create brand new Design or FigJam files from scratch
  • Read and edit any file you give it the URL or file key to
  • Create and modify elements and layers inside a file (basically, it can design for you)

What it can’t do:

  • Browse your Figma workspace or list your files
  • Rename existing files
  • Move files between projects
  • See anything about your Figma profile or history
I cancelled my ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini subscriptions for Claude — and I should have sooner

Wish I did this sooner.

51
By  Mahnoor Faisal

What I actually use this integration for

And how I get the most out of it

The most useful thing about the FigJam side of the integration isn’t the diagramming itself, but what you can throw at it. I’ve pushed a typed-out wall of notes, screenshots of competitors’ onboarding flow, and a document of the product requirements and asked Claude to map it all into a user flow, and it just does it. It shows up as a preview in the chat first so you can keep tweaking it before it lands in FigJam.

And then there’s Figma Design. This part is basically vibe-coding but for design files. You describe your screen - layout, theme, the elements you need, and so on - and Claude builds it as actual native Figma layers, frames and all. The trick is being more specific than feels necessary, unless you’re just experimenting or short on ideas. But I recommend feeding it your design brief and any other notes or reference materials. It’s not going to be a finished design either way, the point is for it to act as a template of sorts so you can iterate on your own.

And lastly, I also use this integration to point Claude at what I’ve already designed and get feedback. The get_design_context tool pulls a screenshot of the node plus all the metadata - it can basically analyze the entire design file, see it visually, and read all its properties, as long as you give it the URL. This is an excellent way to spar with the chatbot about your process, constraints, and ideas. It can pull from its training data, UX research, design conventions, whatever’s relevant, and apply all of it specifically to what it’s looking at in your design file.

Not just for the devs

The Claude and Figma integration has a bit of an image problem - most of the content around it seems aimed at developers, which makes it easy to write off if you can’t code. But the designer-facing side of it is genuinely useful in its own right. FigJam can diagram from messy inputs, you can design files from conversation context, and get feedback grounded in what’s actually in your file. If you’re already on Claude Pro, connecting it to Figma takes just a minute and it’s worth finding out what it can do for your specific workflow.

Claude
OS
Windows, macOS
Individual pricing
Free plan available; $17/month Pro plan
Group pricing
$100/month per person for the Max plan
See at Claude
Figma
See at Figma

The trick is being more specific than feels necessary, unless you’re just experimenting or short on ideas. But I recommend feeding it your design brief and any other notes or reference materials. It’s not going to be a finished design either way, the point is for it to act as a template of sorts so you can iterate on your own.

And lastly, I also use this integration to point Claude at what I’ve already designed and get feedback. The get_design_context tool pulls a screenshot of the node plus all the metadata - it can basically analyze the entire design file, see it visually, and read all its properties, as long as you give it the URL. This is an excellent way to spar with the chatbot about your process, constraints, and ideas. It can pull from its training data, UX research, design conventions, whatever’s relevant, and apply all of it specifically to what it’s looking at in your design file.

Not just for the devs

The Claude and Figma integration has a bit of an image problem - most of the content around it seems aimed at developers, which makes it easy to write off if you can’t code. But the designer-facing side of it is genuinely useful in its own right. FigJam can diagram from messy inputs, you can design files from conversation context, and get feedback grounded in what’s actually in your file. If you’re already on Claude Pro, connecting it to Figma takes just a minute and it’s worth finding out what it can do for your specific workflow.

Claude
OS
Windows, macOS
Individual pricing
Free plan available; $17/month Pro plan
Group pricing
$100/month per person for the Max plan
See at Claude
Figma
See at Figma
item.content:encoded:
As someone who likes to stay up to date with the design world, I’ve seen a lot of content around integrating Claude with Figma lately. And it honestly feels like it’s primarily aimed at people who code - as someone who doesn’t code at all, I felt a bit left out. Setting up MCP servers and bridging your design tokens to a codebase aren’t in my wheelhouse. It’s genuinely useful stuff, but not for someone who uses Figma the way an average designer does.

What I found when I actually started testing the integration is that there’s a whole workflow here that nobody’s really talking about, and it’s built for the non-developer side of the Figma user base. You can push structured diagrams straight to FigJam, and also use Claude to build design files complete with editable layers in the design canvas. It’s a total design collaborator that can reach into your workspace. Here’s why I started using it, and what a workflow like this can actually do for your designs…

Why use Claude with Figma?

When Figma already has its own AI

Figma has been building its own AI pretty aggressively. There’s the First Draft feature for generating UI from a text prompt, text tools for rewriting copy directly in your designs, and the biggest one: Figma Make, which lets you transform design files, images, or text instructions into working prototypes through a conversational AI interface. So why bring Claude into the mix at all when Figma already has AI?

The difference is less about output quality and more about how it lets me actually work. Figma Make is a prompt-in and design-out tool. You describe something, it generates, and then you can iterate on it, which is great for speed and experimentation. But it takes a clean prompt and runs with it, whereas Claude is more of a back-and-forth. You can spend twenty minutes working through a design problem, changing directions multiple times, reference your documents, debate UX decisions, and then say “okay now put that into Figma”. The whole conversation context goes with it, whereas Figma Make doesn’t have that thread.

Claude also handles messy inputs in a way Figma’s AI doesn't. I can get really messy with disjointed ideas, spelling errors, and screenshots that don’t make sense, and Claude just gets me. It’s excellent at inferring what I’m trying to convey and hands me something structured back.

I set up Claude Code the way its creator does, and the difference is night and day

Who better to learn from than the person who made it?

18
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Setting up Figma with Claude

There’s nothing technical about it

Again, I’m not talking about setting up an MCP server here, but simply integrating Figma with a Claude.ai connector. It does use MCP under the hood, but Anthropic hosts and manages it for you. There are a few things worth knowing before getting started: you will need a Claude Pro account, unfortunately, since the connector isn’t usable on the free tier. But a free Figma account is fine though.

In Claude, go to Settings > Connectors, browse the Figma connector, hit Connect, and follow the authentication prompts to grant Claude access to your account. It’ll open a Figma login in the browser, click Allow, and that’s pretty much it. You can configure the permissions if needed. Then it’s just a matter of prompting Claude for whatever you want.

Here’s what you can do with the Claude Figma connector:

  • Create brand new Design or FigJam files from scratch
  • Read and edit any file you give it the URL or file key to
  • Create and modify elements and layers inside a file (basically, it can design for you)

What it can’t do:

  • Browse your Figma workspace or list your files
  • Rename existing files
  • Move files between projects
  • See anything about your Figma profile or history
I cancelled my ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini subscriptions for Claude — and I should have sooner

Wish I did this sooner.

51
By  Mahnoor Faisal

What I actually use this integration for

And how I get the most out of it

The most useful thing about the FigJam side of the integration isn’t the diagramming itself, but what you can throw at it. I’ve pushed a typed-out wall of notes, screenshots of competitors’ onboarding flow, and a document of the product requirements and asked Claude to map it all into a user flow, and it just does it. It shows up as a preview in the chat first so you can keep tweaking it before it lands in FigJam.

And then there’s Figma Design. This part is basically vibe-coding but for design files. You describe your screen - layout, theme, the elements you need, and so on - and Claude builds it as actual native Figma layers, frames and all. The trick is being more specific than feels necessary, unless you’re just experimenting or short on ideas. But I recommend feeding it your design brief and any other notes or reference materials. It’s not going to be a finished design either way, the point is for it to act as a template of sorts so you can iterate on your own.

And lastly, I also use this integration to point Claude at what I’ve already designed and get feedback. The get_design_context tool pulls a screenshot of the node plus all the metadata - it can basically analyze the entire design file, see it visually, and read all its properties, as long as you give it the URL. This is an excellent way to spar with the chatbot about your process, constraints, and ideas. It can pull from its training data, UX research, design conventions, whatever’s relevant, and apply all of it specifically to what it’s looking at in your design file.

Not just for the devs

The Claude and Figma integration has a bit of an image problem - most of the content around it seems aimed at developers, which makes it easy to write off if you can’t code. But the designer-facing side of it is genuinely useful in its own right. FigJam can diagram from messy inputs, you can design files from conversation context, and get feedback grounded in what’s actually in your file. If you’re already on Claude Pro, connecting it to Figma takes just a minute and it’s worth finding out what it can do for your specific workflow.

Claude

Windows, macOS
