item.title: Every Windows user should know about God Mode, and it still works in Windows 11
item.itunes:title: Every Windows user should know about God Mode, and it still works in Windows 11
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Windows has always had this strange split personality. On the one hand, you get the modern Settings app that looks clean but often hides deeper controls. On the other hand, you still have layers of legacy menus and Control Panel pages that haven't gone away. If you've used Windows 11 long enough, you've probably felt this disconnect every time you try to change something specific and end up clicking through five different menus. And that's exactly why God Mode, a feature that dates back to Windows Vista, still matters for Windows users.

For those who don't know, it's not some unique mode that unlocks secret features. It's just a special folder that brings together hundreds of system settings into one place, and that alone makes a bigger difference than you'd expect. Instead of jumping between Settings, Control Panel, and random pop-up windows, you get a single, organized list that takes you straight to what you need. Once you get the hang of it, you'll realize how much friction you’ve been dealing with every time you navigate Windows the usual way.

Windows 11 buries its best features for power-users deep behind the worst menus

The power is there, but you shouldn't have to be an archaeologist to find it

1
By  Samarveer Singh

You're just creating a new folder

It only takes seconds to set up, and that's the least interesting part

If setting up God Mode were complicated, it wouldn't really be worth your time or mine. But that's not the case here. You’re not installing anything or digging through obscure system menus to enable it. You just create a new folder, rename it with a specific string, and Windows immediately turns it into something far more useful than it looks. For reference, here's the string you need to use:

GodMode.{ED7BA470-8E54-465E-825C-99712043E01C}

Upon renaming this, you'll see that the folder icon instantly changes, and it no longer behaves like a regular folder. Opening it reveals a long, categorized list of system settings that are usually scattered across different parts of Windows. There's nothing else you really need to do besides moving the folder to your desired location. If you want to pin it to the Start menu or taskbar, however, you'll need to create a new shortcut and use this string instead:

explorer shell:::{ED7BA470-8E54-465E-825C-99712043E01C}

You can name this "God Mode" or anything you want, and from there, you can treat it just like any other shortcut. What actually matters is everything you get access to in one place. You don't have to think about where a specific setting is anymore. You just scroll or search and go straight to it. And that's really the whole point here. Not how easy it is to set up, but how much friction it removes once you get used to it.

God Mode shows settings that Windows still buries

You don't have to think about whether a menu is in Settings or Control Panel

It's been nearly half a decade since Microsoft rolled out Windows 11, but it still hasn't fully moved on from its older design. Sure, everything looks and feels modern at a glance, but that changes the moment you try to find something specific. Some options live in the modern Settings app, while others are still tucked away in the outdated Control Panel. And that fragmentation is what slows everything down. Even if you've used Windows for years, you still end up second-guessing where something might be.

Instead of wondering whether you have to check Settings first or Control Panel, you can use God Mode and skip that entire process. Any setting that would've otherwise required you to dig through multiple menus is right there in front of you. For instance, if you want to access advanced mouse settings, there's no need to navigate through the Settings app first. You just click exactly what you want under the Mouse section of the God Mode folder. Every legacy setting you access as a long-time Windows user is just one click away.

Windows Search makes God Mode feel redundant

But what do you do when you don’t know what to search for?

You'd be quick to argue that God Mode seems unnecessary when you have Windows Search right at your fingertips. And to be fair, I get your point. For most common tasks, search works exactly how you’d expect. You could type in "mouse settings" and click the result to get straight to that specific part in the Settings app. It's more direct, and you don't have to think about where anything really is. But that becomes a problem when you don't know what to look for in the first place.

Subscribe for Windows tips - unlock God Mode

Join the newsletter for clear, actionable Windows guidance: step-by-step setup for features like God Mode, where buried settings live, and practical tips to reduce the friction of fragmented Settings and Control Panel navigation.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

If you only have a vague idea of what you want to change, Windows Search starts feeling more like guesswork. You could try a few keywords and land in the wrong place. And at that point, you're right back to digging through menus, anyway. God Mode, on the other hand, feels overwhelming at first, but it's actually more predictable once you start using it regularly. You scroll through the categories, and most of the time, what you need is already there. And when you use it often, it becomes second nature, which search can't really replicate when you're still figuring out what you need.

Windows 11's fragmented menus keep God Mode relevant

Even though Windows has come a long way in how it looks and feels, the settings menus are still all over the place. Microsoft still hasn't properly addressed this issue, and as long as that fragmentation exists, the God Mode folder will continue to make sense for a lot of users. It might seem pointless at first when you have Windows Search, but once you get familiar with the layout and where things are, it becomes a far easier way to navigate Windows. At the end of the day, it's all about reducing the friction that Windows still hasn't solved, and that's exactly why it still matters.

6 features worth enabling God Mode in Windows 11 for

If you want access to over 200 features in Windows 11 without complex navigation, use these features in God Mode.

By  Brian Burgess

item.title: Stop upgrading your CPU for media server transcoding; a used GPU does it better
item.itunes:title: Stop upgrading your CPU for media server transcoding; a used GPU does it better
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
My homelab Debian server runs on an old PC, hosting containerized services via Docker on Portainer, including Jellyfin. The server didn't have a dedicated GPU, so Jellyfin relied entirely on CPU transcoding. Whenever someone tried streaming remotely, my server sounded like it was preparing for a takeoff. Even when I streamed a media file on mobile at lower resolution within the network, the server‘s CPU usage would spike to 90%+. It made my other services lag or become unresponsive.

That’s when I started looking for a solution. My first instinct was to upgrade the CPU or maybe build a new server. But the obvious solution came with its own hurdles. If I were to upgrade the CPU, I also had to upgrade the motherboard. A full upgrade server was unreasonable given the costs of new components. Then I started researching whether a cheap used GPU could solve my problem without spending too much on new hardware. I found out that transcoding doesn’t depend on raw GPU power. A $50 old GPU could outperform a $200 modern CPU, not because of raw specs, but because of its dedicated video encoders.

I turned a dead GPU into a hardware encoder, and it's perfect for my NAS

Waste not want not

15
By  Joe Rice-Jones

Your GPU has a hidden encoder that still holds up

It was never about raw power

The natural assumption about GPUs is simple: a better GPU translates to better gaming performance. It is the right assumption, but that assumption doesn't hold in the case of transcoding. Transcoding uses a different part of the GPU. Gaming performance relies on CUDA cores and shaders, whereas video encoding and decoding rely on video engines, such as NVENC for Nvidia or VCE for AMD.

Gaming performance of a GPU degrades with time, as modern games require more raw power, whereas video codecs evolve much slower. An old 2016 GPU encoder can handle similar transcoding workloads as newer GPUs because H.264 and HEVC standards haven’t changed. A GTX 1050 and a GTX 1080 can perform similarly in transcoding because they share the same NVENC generation.

In real-world use, an old GTX 1050 can still do 2-3 simultaneous 1080p to 720p transcodes with reduced bitrates for remote streaming in Jellyfin. This was my ideal use case too. I typically have one or two streams daily, and a GPU can handle these easily. With the GPU addition, other services wouldn't be affected while media is being streamed. An old GTX 1050 is not suitable for scenarios like 4K HDR tone mapping, AV1 encoding, or high bitrate HEVC transcoding. If I were planning these, I would require a newer card.

Why this can save your entire build budget

The upgrade you don’t need

Upgrading the CPU isn’t as simple as it sounds. On the one hand, I would get a modern CPU, and on the other hand, it will come with a number of compatibility issues. The CPU upgrade won’t just be a CPU upgrade; the new CPU has to be compatible with the motherboard, and if I upgrade the motherboard, I’d need to verify RAM compatibility too.

With a GPU, the upgrade is far simpler; I am mostly paying for the GPU and maybe a PSU upgrade if I were running something underpowered for the GPU. The GTX 1050 takes around 75 watts and the GTX 1080 around 180 watts at peak gaming usage, but for transcoding, both use far less. In my case, I’m already safe, as even under load, the transcoding only uses 15–30 watts at most, well within my PSU’s capacity.

Beyond the cost advantages, GPU transcoding solves CPU performance bottlenecks too. Under load, CPU usage spikes to 90%+, which affects other services too. With GPU transcoding, the CPU stays free to handle other container workloads.

For a server that runs 24/7, power consumption is also a big concern. CPU transcoding would consume power near its TDP, ~65 watts for older chips, but offloading the heavy transcoding load to the GPU drops that load significantly. The GPU encoding adds only 15-20 watts.

This $50 GPU upgrade makes more sense than a $200 full platform upgrade.

When you actually should upgrade instead

This is where it falls apart

This approach of getting an old GPU works for current workloads, but it has clear limits. If you need to transcode AV1 media files, old cards are useless, and you would require at least an RTX 40-series or RX 7000-series. Older cards struggle when you try to stream multiple 4K streams simultaneously or high-bitrate 4K HEVC transcodes. Older cards like the GTX 1080 can only handle 1 or 2 light 4K streams at most, but beyond that, it falls apart. We can’t even think of 4K HDR tone mapping in these GPUs; either they won’t support it or produce washed-out colors and artifacting.

Subscribe to our newsletter for smart homelab upgrades

Gain deeper coverage: subscribe to the newsletter for practical homelab hardware tradeoffs, step-by-step GPU transcoding guidance, and budget upgrade strategies that help you decide when to add a used GPU versus replacing your platform.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

If you are on an Intel platform, a 12th gen+ CPU upgrade can be surprisingly better than adding an old GPU. Modern Intel processors have Intel Quick Sync that supports AV1, has lower power consumption, and handles tone mapping better than older NVENC generations. My old PC doesn’t support modern Quick Sync, so a dedicated GPU makes sense for me.

On the software side, older GPUs can be finicky with newer Linux kernels, especially AMD on older GCN cards. There are a few hardware acceleration quirks on Jellyfin and Plex, such as Docker passthrough, ffmpeg builds, and permissions, but they are solvable. It isn’t always plug-and-play with dGPUs, and it is not a dealbreaker for most setups.

5 reasons to overclock your GPU instead of buying a new one

Consider overclocking your GPU before rushing for another GPU upgrade

2
By  Tanveer Singh

Upgrade only when your workload demands it

It is always easier to assume that better performance only comes from newer hardware. That’s true for gaming, as new games demand more raw power, but it doesn’t apply to transcoding because video codecs don’t evolve at that rate. The same dedicated encoders on a 2016 GPU can still handle today’s video codecs just fine. In my setup, the difference between upgrading the whole CPU platform versus adding an old GPU was significant. So a cheap used GPU made more sense than the CPU.

My homelab Debian server runs on an old PC, hosting containerized services via Docker on Portainer, including Jellyfin. The server didn't have a dedicated GPU, so Jellyfin relied entirely on CPU transcoding. Whenever someone tried streaming remotely, my server sounded like it was preparing for a takeoff. Even when I streamed a media file on mobile at lower resolution within the network, the server‘s CPU usage would spike to 90%+. It made my other services lag or become unresponsive.

That’s when I started looking for a solution. My first instinct was to upgrade the CPU or maybe build a new server. But the obvious solution came with its own hurdles. If I were to upgrade the CPU, I also had to upgrade the motherboard. A full upgrade server was unreasonable given the costs of new components. Then I started researching whether a cheap used GPU could solve my problem without spending too much on new hardware. I found out that transcoding doesn’t depend on raw GPU power. A $50 old GPU could outperform a $200 modern CPU, not because of raw specs, but because of its dedicated video encoders.

I turned a dead GPU into a hardware encoder, and it's perfect for my NAS

Waste not want not

15
By  Joe Rice-Jones

Your GPU has a hidden encoder that still holds up

It was never about raw power

The natural assumption about GPUs is simple: a better GPU translates to better gaming performance. It is the right assumption, but that assumption doesn't hold in the case of transcoding. Transcoding uses a different part of the GPU. Gaming performance relies on CUDA cores and shaders, whereas video encoding and decoding rely on video engines, such as NVENC for Nvidia or VCE for AMD.

Gaming performance of a GPU degrades with time, as modern games require more raw power, whereas video codecs evolve much slower. An old 2016 GPU encoder can handle similar transcoding workloads as newer GPUs because H.264 and HEVC standards haven’t changed. A GTX 1050 and a GTX 1080 can perform similarly in transcoding because they share the same NVENC generation.

In real-world use, an old GTX 1050 can still do 2-3 simultaneous 1080p to 720p transcodes with reduced bitrates for remote streaming in Jellyfin. This was my ideal use case too. I typically have one or two streams daily, and a GPU can handle these easily. With the GPU addition, other services wouldn't be affected while media is being streamed. An old GTX 1050 is not suitable for scenarios like 4K HDR tone mapping, AV1 encoding, or high bitrate HEVC transcoding. If I were planning these, I would require a newer card.

Why this can save your entire build budget

The upgrade you don’t need

Upgrading the CPU isn’t as simple as it sounds. On the one hand, I would get a modern CPU, and on the other hand, it will come with a number of compatibility issues. The CPU upgrade won’t just be a CPU upgrade; the new CPU has to be compatible with the motherboard, and if I upgrade the motherboard, I’d need to verify RAM compatibility too.

With a GPU, the upgrade is far simpler; I am mostly paying for the GPU and maybe a PSU upgrade if I were running something underpowered for the GPU. The GTX 1050 takes around 75 watts and the GTX 1080 around 180 watts at peak gaming usage, but for transcoding, both use far less. In my case, I’m already safe, as even under load, the transcoding only uses 15–30 watts at most, well within my PSU’s capacity.

Beyond the cost advantages, GPU transcoding solves CPU performance bottlenecks too. Under load, CPU usage spikes to 90%+, which affects other services too. With GPU transcoding, the CPU stays free to handle other container workloads.

For a server that runs 24/7, power consumption is also a big concern. CPU transcoding would consume power near its TDP, ~65 watts for older chips, but offloading the heavy transcoding load to the GPU drops that load significantly. The GPU encoding adds only 15-20 watts.

This $50 GPU upgrade makes more sense than a $200 full platform upgrade.

When you actually should upgrade instead

This is where it falls apart

This approach of getting an old GPU works for current workloads, but it has clear limits. If you need to transcode AV1 media files, old cards are useless, and you would require at least an RTX 40-series or RX 7000-series. Older cards struggle when you try to stream multiple 4K streams simultaneously or high-bitrate 4K HEVC transcodes. Older cards like the GTX 1080 can only handle 1 or 2 light 4K streams at most, but beyond that, it falls apart. We can’t even think of 4K HDR tone mapping in these GPUs; either they won’t support it or produce washed-out colors and artifacting.

Subscribe to our newsletter for smart homelab upgrades

Gain deeper coverage: subscribe to the newsletter for practical homelab hardware tradeoffs, step-by-step GPU transcoding guidance, and budget upgrade strategies that help you decide when to add a used GPU versus replacing your platform.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

If you are on an Intel platform, a 12th gen+ CPU upgrade can be surprisingly better than adding an old GPU. Modern Intel processors have Intel Quick Sync that supports AV1, has lower power consumption, and handles tone mapping better than older NVENC generations. My old PC doesn’t support modern Quick Sync, so a dedicated GPU makes sense for me.

On the software side, older GPUs can be finicky with newer Linux kernels, especially AMD on older GCN cards. There are a few hardware acceleration quirks on Jellyfin and Plex, such as Docker passthrough, ffmpeg builds, and permissions, but they are solvable. It isn’t always plug-and-play with dGPUs, and it is not a dealbreaker for most setups.

5 reasons to overclock your GPU instead of buying a new one

Consider overclocking your GPU before rushing for another GPU upgrade

2
By  Tanveer Singh

Upgrade only when your workload demands it

It is always easier to assume that better performance only comes from newer hardware. That’s true for gaming, as new games demand more raw power, but it doesn’t apply to transcoding because video codecs don’t evolve at that rate. The same dedicated encoders on a 2016 GPU can still handle today’s video codecs just fine. In my setup, the difference between upgrading the whole CPU platform versus adding an old GPU was significant. So a cheap used GPU made more sense than the CPU.

item.title: Building your own router is easier than you think, and the US just made it necessary
item.itunes:title: Building your own router is easier than you think, and the US just made it necessary
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
It's really easy to get the idea that routers are something you build, not make. PCs you can construct with parts, and you can even make your own laptop if you're dedicated enough, but when it comes to routers, people often opt to purchase one instead of going DIY.

However, if you're living in the US, making your own router just got a lot more feasible compared to purchasing one. In a bid to get people producing routers locally, the US government has recently banned new routers from overseas from getting the proper qualifications to sell in the US. So, if you're worried about rising prices or not finding a model that suits your needs, why not build your own?

The US government have banned new routers made outside the US...

The country may not see new routers from other countries being sold in its borders for a while

Back in March 23rd, the US government announced that it was banning new routers produced outside the US. The goal was both to inspire electronics companies in the US to make routers on American soil and also protect the country's infrastructure from malicious agents.

The National Security Determination made a statement about the decision:

“Recently, malicious state and non-state sponsored cyber attackers have increasingly leveraged the vulnerabilities in small and home office routers produced abroad to carry out direct attacks against American civilians in their homes. [...] Routers in the United States must have trusted supply chains so we are not providing foreign actors with a built-in backdoor to American homes, businesses, critical infrastructure, and emergency services.”

It's worth noting that this only prevents new routers being sold from overseas companies from being sold on US soil. If you own a router that originated from an overseas country, the law won't affect you, and you can continue to use it. Also, foreign companies can register their interest with the US to sell their routers in America, but they have to show proof that they'll help move their production line over to the US and begin making routers there.

Setting up OPNsense on a mini PC was easier than configuring my old router

Consistent performance with predictable behavior

2
By  Samir Makwana

...but that doesn't stop you from making your own

It's not too complicated, either

While it's still way too early to see the impact this ruling has had on the router market, there is one way you can avoid being caught up in any mass panic-buying people may perform as routers become a more scarce commodity. The idea is that you forgo purchasing your next router and instead build your own.

Before you envision yourself hunched over a workdesk trying to solder together something that looks like a T-Link router, it's actually a lot easier than it sounds. Despite them looking drastically different to a PC on an inside, a router is, essentially, just a computer on the inside. And there's absolutely nothing stopping you from grabbing a PC, setting it up as a router, and using it as a daily driver.

If you're looking to turn a PC into a router, you could always give your old computers a second life and have them manage your network. However, DIY router hobbyists often purchase a mini PC that checks all the boxes that people look for in a networking device.

Setting up a local DNS was one of the easiest improvements I made to my home network

Having local control opens up so many possibilities

14
By  Joe Rice-Jones

There's plenty to consider when making your own router

Do some research before you hop in

Turning a computer into a router takes a little more nuance than using it as a workstation PC. It has to check a few boxes for it to be suitable for being converted into a router.

For one, you're going to need at least two NICs. One of them will handle your WAN connection, and the other, your LAN. You can use either Intel or Realtek for the job, but people often find that Intel NICs play better with the software and drivers required for a DIY router, so aim for those if possible.

For the CPU, you don't need something bleeding edge, but you will need something that's moderately powerful to handle all your traffic. The holy grail in the DIY router community is the Intel N100; it doesn't use a ton of power, it can handle 2.5GbE routing just fine, and it's pretty cheap.

Subscribe to our newsletter for DIY router guides

Joining our newsletter gives you detailed DIY router walkthroughs, parts checklists, configuration tips, and beginner mistakes to avoid — plus practical coverage of related networking and hardware topics to help you build or repurpose a PC as a secure hom
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

For RAM, you'll want 4GB minimum, with 8GB being the sweet spot. If you want to really push your router to the edge, 16GB isn't unheard of, but it can be overkill if you're just using it to browse the web on one device.

We love our DIY routers here at XDA, so here are some resources you can check out as a beginner:

  • I replaced my ISP's router with a cheap mini PC — and you can too
  • 5 mistakes I made with my first mini PC router that you can avoid
  • 5 ways building my own router improved my home network
I never set up a home network without these 3 firewall rules

Default deny.

6
By  Rich Edmonds

Escape the potential panic by making your own router

With the US government banning new foreign routers from being sold in the US, now's the perfect time to consider making your own. While there's no guarantee that the router market will go turbulent in the future, having the option of going the DIY route gives you a safety net against the worst case scenario.
